behaviors.description: The traveling-salesman heuristic produces a route that is no more than 1.5x the length of the optimal route for inputs up to 20 stops.
behaviors.justification: This is a pure algorithmic test with no external I/O or mocking needed. The failure boundary is within the heuristic's computation logic: it either produces good-quality routes or doesn't. Real failure: the algorithm produces routes significantly worse than 1.5x optimal. A Unit test executes the actual heuristic code on known benchmark instances (TSPLIB, synthetic grids) with 5-20 stops and measures the solution quality. No mock can replace this—you need to run the actual algorithm and evaluate its output.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test fixture with standard TSP benchmark instances (small TSPLIB problems and synthetic coordinate grids) with 5-20 stops, each with a known optimal or near-optimal solution length. For each instance, run the heuristic, compute the actual route length, and assert it is ≤ 1.5× the optimal. Test multiple instance types (clustered, grid, random) to verify robustness.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test description directly exercises the heuristic code and measures its output quality—no mocking, no external calls, purely in-process computation. This is the core boundary where the failure lives.

behaviors.description: The delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from the distance between consecutive stops and the configured average speed for the road type.
behaviors.justification: This is pure calculation logic that should use the formula: time = distance / avg_speed[road_type]. No external dependencies, no I/O, no mocking needed. Real failure: the formula is wrong (off-by-one, unit mismatch, wrong speed lookup), or the configuration is misread. A Unit test provides known distances and road types, calls the computation function, and verifies the result. A mock cannot add value here—you need the actual function to run with real test data.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test with parametrized inputs: specific distance values (10 km, 50 km, 100 km) and road types (highway, local, city). For each combination, provide the configured average speeds and verify time = distance / avg_speed is computed correctly. Include edge cases like very short distances and mismatched road types.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test directly calls the estimation function with hardcoded inputs and expected outputs. No external state, no mocking, purely formula verification. Failure is caught at the function boundary.

behaviors.description: The shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount.
behaviors.justification: This is a simple comparison and arithmetic check: if total_time > shift_hours_remaining, reject and return excess. No external state, no I/O, no mocking. Real failure: wrong comparison operator, unit mismatch, or excess calculation error. A Unit test provides known route duration and shift hours, calls the validator, and checks the result.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test with explicit input scenarios: route duration = 8 hours, remaining shift = 6 hours (should reject with excess = 2h); route duration = 4 hours, remaining shift = 6 hours (should accept); edge case where duration exactly equals shift hours (should accept). Verify return value and message clarity.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test directly invokes the validator with known inputs and checks outputs. Failure is purely in the comparison and arithmetic logic, which executes synchronously with no external dependencies.

behaviors.description: When one address in the list fails geocoding (API returns no results), the service skips that stop and optimizes the remaining addresses, reporting which addresses were skipped.
behaviors.justification: This behavior involves multiple modules working together: address processor, geocoder (with mocked API), TSP optimizer, and reporter. A Unit test with an isolated mock would miss failures in how these modules integrate. Real failure: the skip logic works, but the optimizer doesn't run on the remaining set; or the report is missing; or the partial failure causes a downstream crash. To catch the integration of skip + optimize + report, you need Integration-level testing. A mock API is sufficient (no real external call), but you must run the full pipeline and verify the entire workflow handles the partial failure correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test of the full service pipeline: provide an input list of 5 addresses (e.g., four real addresses + one that will fail). Mock the geocoding API to return coordinates for 4 addresses and 'no results' for the 5th. Run the service end-to-end and verify: (a) the failed address is skipped, (b) the TSP optimizer runs on the remaining 4 addresses (not 5), (c) the report lists the skipped address name. Test with different positions of the failed address (first, middle, last) to catch off-by-one errors.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test spans multiple modules (processor → geocoder → optimizer → reporter) and verifies their interaction under partial failure. A Unit test of the skip logic in isolation would not catch failures in how the skipped set is passed to the optimizer or report generator. The mock API is sufficient (not System-level), but the multi-module integration is the failure boundary.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in the input list are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice.
behaviors.justification: Deduplication happens before geocoding, so no external API calls are involved. This is pure logic: detect duplicates, merge items, produce a deduplicated list. Real failure: duplicates not detected (address comparison fails), items not combined, or the optimizer still visits duplicates twice. A Unit test with test data (addresses and delivery items) exercises the dedup logic directly and verifies the output. No mocking or integration is needed.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test with explicit test data: an input list with duplicates (e.g., '123 Main St' appears 3 times with different delivery item counts). Verify the deduplicated output has exactly 1 entry for that address with combined item count. Test edge cases: case-insensitive matching, whitespace normalization (if applicable), and ensure the optimizer is called with the deduplicated set, not the original.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test directly invokes the deduplication logic with controlled test data and verifies the output structure. No external state, no mocking, purely data transformation. Failure is caught within the dedup function boundary.
